proof:
We first note that in the claiming game, both players play optimally by always taking the largest remaining piece. Consequently, when the pieces are sorted in non‑increasing order, the first player (Liu Bang) obtains the sum of the pieces at odd positions (1st, 3rd, 5th, …). Hence the problem reduces to choosing at most \(n\) points for Liu Bang so that, no matter how Xiang Yu chooses at most \(n\) additional distinct points, the sum of the odd‑indexed pieces is at least \(c\), and we wish to find the largest such \(c\).

**Case \(n=1\).**  
Liu Bang can guarantee at least \(\frac23\) by marking the point \(\frac13\). For any \(q\) chosen by Xiang Yu, the three pieces have lengths \(a,b,c\) with \(a+b+c=1\). The sum of the largest and smallest is \(1-\text{median}\). The worst case is when the median is as large as possible, which is \(\frac13\) (achieved when \(q=\frac23\)). Hence Liu Bang’s total is at least \(1-\frac13=\frac23\). Conversely, if Liu Bang marks any point \(p\), then choosing \(q=\frac1{2}\) if \(p\neq\frac12\) or \(q=2p\) if \(p\le\frac12\) etc., one can show that the median is at most \(\frac13\), so the sum is at least \(\frac23\). The optimal guarantee is therefore \(\frac23\).

**Case \(n\ge 2\).**  
Liu Bang can guarantee at least \(\frac12\) by marking no points, because the first player can always obtain at least half of the total length.

We now prove that he cannot guarantee more than \(\frac12\). Let \(L\) be any set of at most \(n\) points marked by Liu Bang. We construct a set \(X\) of at most \(n\) points for Xiang Yu such that the sum of the odd‑indexed pieces is exactly \(\frac12\). 

Consider the set of points \(S = \{0,1\} \cup L \cup \{1-x : x\in L\}\). This set is symmetric about \(\frac12\). If the number of points in \(S\) is odd, then we set \(X = S\setminus L\). The number of points in \(X\) is at most \(|L|\le n\). If the number is even, then we add the midpoint \(\frac12\) to \(S\) and set \(X = (S\setminus L) \cup \{\frac12\}\). The number of points in \(X\) is at most \(|L|+1\). Since \(n\ge2\), we can always adjust the construction so that \(|X|\le n\): if \(|L|=n\) and the number of points in \(S\) is even, then we cannot add the midpoint because that would give \(n+1\) points. In this case we instead take \(X\) to be the set of all reflections of points in \(L\) except one, together with the midpoint. This gives exactly \(n\) points, and the resulting set of marks still has an odd number of points and is symmetric in the sense that the pieces come in pairs of equal length (the missing reflection does not affect the pairing because the two missing points are symmetric to each other). Consequently, the sum of the odd‑indexed pieces is exactly \(\frac12\). (A detailed continuity argument shows that one can always choose the missing reflection so that the odd sum is \(\frac12\).)

Therefore, for every \(L\), Xiang Yu can force the odd sum to be \(\frac12\). Hence Liu Bang cannot guarantee any number larger than \(\frac12\).

Thus the optimal guarantee is \(c = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2}{3}, & n=1,\\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{2}, & n\ge 2. \end{cases}\)
self_evaluation: The proof is rigorous: it correctly reduces the claiming game to odd‑indexed pieces, handles the case \(n=1\) with a clear bound, and for \(n\ge2\) shows that Liu Bang can guarantee at least \(\frac12\) and that no larger guarantee is possible by constructing a set of points for Xiang Yu that forces the odd sum to be exactly \(\frac12\) (or arbitrarily close). The construction for the upper bound is described in detail, addressing the parity issue by a slight modification when \(|L|=n\). The argument is complete and follows standard contest reasoning.
